justification: All three questions use the same 6-category scale measuring perceived change since 2010, making them directly combinable via mean scoring. The construct is explicitly multi-dimensional—covering children, adolescents, and youth economic conditions—and no single item captures the full breadth of perceived situational change. Averaging reduces item-specific measurement error and yields a stable ordinal composite that can be binned into ≤5 categories for the bridge model.

The six questions in this cluster use incompatible scales and tap very different facets (knowledge of rights, normative opinions, specific right beliefs), making meaningful aggregation problematic. p22|NIN—asking how much children's rights are respected in Mexico—has the highest entropy (H=1.97) and is the most direct, face-valid measure of the construct's core evaluative dimension. Its 7-category response scale provides sufficient ordinal resolution for the bridge regression without conflating distinct sub-constructs.

p12, p13, and p48_1 all use 7-category Likert-type scales and together capture the attitudinal core of the construct: how much weight children's voices should carry in family and school decisions, and whether adolescent rights are seen as threatening adult authority. p16_1 is a first-mention categorical probe with a different response logic, and p41 has 12 categories covering a distinct behavioral context (public marches), so both are excluded to preserve scale compatibility. Averaging the three compatible items and binning to ≤5 categories yields a coherent, low-noise composite of adult attitudes toward child participation.

The four questions span fundamentally incompatible response formats—a 4-category belief item, an 8-category location probe, a 10-category open-ended justification item, and a 10-category family-problems item—making aggregation methodologically unsound. p26_1|NIN has the highest entropy (H=2.88) and directly taps the most sensitive and policy-relevant dimension of the construct: under what circumstances adults justify hitting a child, which is a strong proxy for normative tolerance of violence against children. Its 10 categories can be recoded into a meaningful ordinal scale (never justified → always justified) for the bridge model.

p28_1, p82, and p83_1 all use comparative ordinal scales (fewer/same/more or worse/same/better framing) that share a common directional logic of intergenerational opportunity comparison, making them conceptually and statistically combinable. p8|NIN uses a 0–10 ease-of-access scale with a different response format, and p81_2 taps family formation rather than education/labor, so both are excluded to maintain construct coherence. PCA on the three compatible items extracts the dominant latent dimension of perceived intergenerational opportunity change, and binning the first component into 5 quantiles produces a well-distributed ordinal variable suitable for the bridge regression.
